feed.title: SAP Community - SAP Integration Suite
feed.subtitle: SAP Integration Suite Q&A in SAP Community

entry.title: 401 in Integration Suite - unable to access JMS
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

My integration Suite suddenly unable to show the JMS queue facility.  I already done relogging and clear cache, i also use incognito. I am not sure where to fix this.

entry.title: SAP CPI OAuth2 Client Credentials failing with 400 error — how to fix?
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I’m trying to use OAuth2 Client Credentials authentication in SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), but I’m getting an 400 error when the iFlow tries to fetch the token. I’d appreciate guidance on what might be misconfigured and how to resolve it.

I'm attaching the screenshots of all the configurations.

1. OAuth2 Client Credentials configured in security materials.

I'm calling this in HTTP to fetch token.

I'm also passing Content-Type =  application/x-www-form-urlencoded in header in HTTP adapter.

2. I can see Authorization in Runlogs with Bearer ***

3. Getting 400 error asking for 'grant-_type' in response payload.

P.s : please correct me if I'm wrong, while using OAuth2 Client Credentials, by default "grant_type" is "client_credentials" and it goes in body.

entry.title: Integration Suite Access
entry.content: I am facing this while accesing the created integration suite in BTP. Also, integration provisioner role was assigned and relogged in to check if it is accessible, still no.

entry.title: CPI logic to ensure that two files are sent together in a single request.
entry.content:
Hello Experts, 

In our project for Ariba Remittance, the requirement is to send two interdependent files together as a single bundle. Currently, CPI is sending these files one by one, which is not aligned with Ariba’s expectation. 

we require guidance specifically on the CPI logic to ensure that both files are sent together in a single request. 

Thanks in advance. 

Regards, VS

entry.title: Asset transfer within the co code depreciation issue
entry.content:
• Formwork has been purchased on multiple dates.
  • There have been several transfers made within the profit centres based on requirements
  • Depreciation is being calculated on a weighted average basis based on purchase date of each individual lot
  • Due to these transfers, the depreciation calculation is impacting individual projects depreciation expense.
  • However, the overall depreciation percentage remains consistent.
  • Kindly advise a suitable mechanism to  be carried out to rectify the depreciation discrepancies. Using the T code ABUMN

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite Runtime APIs
entry.content:
Hello Team,

I am currently working on leveraging SAP Integration Suite Runtime APIs and have been exploring available documentation and resources.

To ensure alignment with SAP-recommended approaches and avoid any deviations from best practices, I would like to request your guidance on the following areas:

  1. Official References
    • Links to authentic and up-to-date SAP documentation for Runtime APIs.
    • Any relevant API specifications or SAP Notes that cover this topic.
  2. Recommended Approach
    • Best practices for consuming Runtime APIs in enterprise scenarios.
    • Guidance on authentication strategies, error handling, and scalability considerations.
  3. Implementation Insights
    • Any sample use cases, integration patterns, or reference implementations.
    • Recommendations based on typical customer usage.
  4. Configuration Considerations
    • Key prerequisites, roles, and configuration steps to ensure correct setup.
    • Any known limitations or important considerations.
  5. Troubleshooting & Monitoring
    • Common challenges observed and suggested resolutions.
    • Recommended approach for logging, monitoring, and diagnostics.

I have already started reviewing the available materials but wanted to validate my approach against SAP's recommended standards and see if there are any additional resources I should refer to.

entry.title: Can a programmatically / AI-generated iFlow ZIP be uploaded into SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
**What I'm trying to do**
Generate a valid iFlow ZIP using AI (without manually designing in the editor) and upload it via *Add → Upload → Single File*.

**ZIP structure used:**
```
META-INF/MANIFEST.MF
src/main/resources/script/SalesOrderMapping.groovy
src/main/resources/scenarioflows/integrationflow/SF_to_S4HANA_SalesOrder.iflw
```
MANIFEST.MF includes `SAP-BundleType: IntegrationFlow`, single file, at ZIP root.

**Error:**
> Upload of the resource failed. Integration Flow project is invalid.

**Already checked:** single MANIFEST, correct root level, no __MACOSX folders, Bundle-SymbolicName matches artifact name.

**Questions:**
1. Is this approach feasible at all?
2. Are there undocumented required files (e.g. `parameter.prop`)?
3. Does `.iflw` need a `bpmndi:BPMNDiagram` layout section to be valid?

Happy to share the full files. Any working minimal ZIP example would be very helpful.

entry.title: SAP CI Connection to Salesforce fails intermittently - works after iFlow redeployment
entry.content:
Hello All,

I am working on an interface in SAP Cloud Integration which involves multiple iFlows connected by ProcessDirect adapter. These iFlows call Salesforce using the Salesforce adapter with Authentication set as "OAuth 2.0 JWT Bearer".

The calls to Salesforce works fine except that they sometimes fail with the following error:

Error: org.apache.camel.CamelException: [{"message":"INVALID_JWT_FORMAT","errorCode":"INVALID_AUTH_HEADER"}]

This happens especially when the iFlow is called from another iFlow using the ProcessDirect adapter. even though both the iFlows have calls to Salesforce using the same authentication method, the calls (intermittently) fails in the consumer iFlow with above error.

Also, it starts working after we deploy the consumer iFlow again. 

I can confirm that the Authentication credentials being used are correct as they also work most of the times. This seems to be some runtime cache related issue which gets resolved after the iFlow is deployed again. Please suggest how could this issue be resolved?

Regards,

Faisal

entry.title: France E-Invoicing Set up from S4H public Cloud Via SAP DRC
entry.content:
Hi Team, 

We need to implement the France E-Invoicing Process from the SAP S4H public cloud via approved platform which is SAP DRC cloud edition. 

We are using the SAP BTP subaccount which is under the region cf-eu10. But the below SAP help document mentioned that  in order to use the Process for the France , cf-eu02 subaccount is required.

Does that mean in Order to use the France E-invoice process cf-eu02 subaccount is mandatory or do we any alternative process that can be used within the cf-eu10?


Also we didn't understand the below note  given the SAP document . Attached the screenshot as well.

Currently you can only connect with the qualification system of the tax authority. For that, you use the test environment of the cloud edition 


SAP help document: Supported Compliance Tasks | SAP Help Portal 




It's on Priority . Please check and confirm 

Regards

Jyothi

entry.title: Unable to see the calls in CPI Monitoring page
entry.content:
Hi Team,
We have 1 flow in CPI which has successfully developed. Flow is APIM- SAP CPI- HR system. We were able to test the flow and we were able to see the calls in HR back end system also. But In CPI monitoring page, we were unable to see the calls. We were able to see the Http logs also. Attached screenshot.

entry.title: Costs related to digital access resulting from integrations with non-SAP applications
entry.content: If I have third-party systems that I want to integrate with SAP S/4HANA Private Edition 2023, I know there are costs associated with Digital Access per document created. Can I save these costs by using an intermediate SAP HANA Cloud table in BTP for synchronous integrations, and for asynchronous integrations, using SFTP via unattended bots? Or could SAP still charge me for Digital Access during the audit?

entry.title: Cloud Integration metering for outbound payloads >250KB does not increase Total Messages
entry.content:
Hello experts,

We are performing controlled transaction metering tests in SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration) and we found a behavior that seems inconsistent with the official documentation regarding payloads larger than 250KB.

Scenario

Integration Flow:

HTTPS Sender → XML to JSON Transformation → HTTP Receiver Adapter → External Node.js API

Additionally, the iFlow performs another outbound HTTP call to an external monitoring API.

The flow does NOT contain:

  • Splitter
  • Multicast
  • JMS
  • DataStore
  • Retries

Test Execution

We executed the test using Postman Runner with:

  • 345 iterations

  • Inbound XML payload size ≈ 690 KB

  • Outbound JSON payload size after transformation ≈ 463 KB

We also verified the following exchange properties:

SAP_InboundMessageSizeInKB = 690
content-length ≈ 689558

Additionally, we logged the outbound payload before the HTTP receiver adapter:

OutboundSizeKB ≈ 463

Expected Behavior

According to SAP documentation:

“The combined (aggregated) size of all outbound transactions via one or more receiver(s) adapter is considered for transaction metering.”

“If it is more than 250KB, it will be counted in blocks of 250KB.”

Based on this rule, we expected:

463KB / 250KB ≈ 2 transactions per execution

Therefore:

345 executions ≈ 690 Total Messages

Observed Behavior

The Message Usage Dashboard shows:

  • MPL Count = 345

  • Total Messages = 345

Meaning:

  • each execution is counted as only 1 message

  • payload size >250KB does not appear to increase the Total Messages counter

Additional Observation

When testing integration flows with Splitter patterns, the Message Usage Dashboard DOES increase Total Messages correctly according to the number of split outbound transactions.

This suggests that:

  • Splitter-based outbound transactions are correctly metered

  • but outbound HTTP payload size >250KB in a simple HTTP receiver scenario may not be metered as expected

Question

Can someone clarify:

  1. Is transaction metering in Cloud Integration really based on outbound payload size blocks of 250KB for HTTP receiver adapters?

  2. Does the Message Usage Dashboard aggregate or simplify payload-based metering?

  3. Is this behavior different in BTP Trial vs productive tenants?

  4. Is the 250KB rule applied only under specific integration patterns?

Any clarification from SAP product experts or architects would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks.

Evidence:

Message Usage Dashboard

 

SAP Inbound & Outbound Size KB

 

iFlow Diagram

 

Splitter Comparison

entry.title: AI-Assisted Development in SAP Cloud Integration: Four Features You Should Know
entry.content:
SAP has been steadily building AI capabilities directly into SAP Integration Suite,         not as a standalone tool, but embedded into the workflows developers use every day. Whether you are creating a new integration flow, modeling individual steps, reviewing Groovy scripts, or connecting to AI services at runtime, there are now AI-powered features available at each of these stages. In this blog post I will walk through four of them that are generally available today in Cloud Integration, explain how each one works, and show you what to expect when you use them.

The features covered in this post:

  • Artifact Generation
  • Flow Design Proposal
  • Script Optimization
  • AI Adapter

 

Artifact Generation

Artifact Generation allows you to create a new Integration Flow by describing your integration scenario in natural language. The AI interprets your description, derives a suitable flow structure, and creates the artifact directly inside your Integration Package — giving you a properly structured starting point without having to build it step by step from an empty canvas.

To access the feature, open an Integration Package and add a new artifact. Instead of jumping straight into an empty editor, you are now presented with two options:

 

 

Select "Generate Integrations with assistance from AI" to open the input dialog. Describe your integration scenario in the text field — it accepts up to 1,000 characters. Descriptions must be written in English; other languages are not supported. The more precise your description is, specifying source system, target system, data format, and the key process steps — the better the result will be.

The AI validates your input immediately. If the description is too vague or does not contain enough information to derive a meaningful flow, it returns a message asking you to refine it. Once the description is sufficient, the AI confirms this, suggests an artifact name, and lets you select the sender and receiver system before generating:

 

 

 

 

 

After clicking Generate, the iFlow is created in the package and opens directly in the Integration Flow Designer. The result is a complete flow structure with all relevant process steps already in place:

 

 

It is important to understand what the AI actually delivers here. The generated iFlow provides the full structural skeleton: correct step sequence, meaningful component names, sender and receiver channels, required converters and loggers, and an Exception Subprocess. What it does not provide is the implementation of any individual step. Adapter configurations, message mappings, Groovy scripts, and error handling logic all still need to be configured manually.

The value of the feature lies in eliminating the setup work at the start of a new integration project: deciding which steps are needed, getting them in the right order, and establishing a consistent naming structure. That groundwork is now done in seconds.

Availability: SAP Integration Suite Premium Edition, not in Standard Edition.

 

Flow Design Proposal

The Flow Design Proposal feature, also referred to as Recommended Steps, provides AI-guided suggestions while you are actively modeling an iFlow. Rather than browsing the full step catalog every time you want to add a component, the AI analyzes the current state of your flow and recommends the most suitable next steps directly in the Add Flow Step dialog:

 

The recommendations appear automatically at the top of the dialog whenever you add a new step. The AI takes into account the adapter types already configured, the message format expected at the current position in the flow, and common design patterns for the type of integration being modeled. Each suggestion includes the component type and a short explanation of why it fits at this point.

Clicking a recommendation inserts the component directly at the correct position in the flow. The suggestions update with every step you add, adapting to the evolving state of the flow.

The full step catalog remains available below the Recommended Steps section at all times the feature is advisory, not restrictive. For experienced developers it speeds up the modeling process; for developers newer to Cloud Integration it provides meaningful guidance on which steps belong in which context.

Availability: SAP Integration Suite Enhanced Edition and on Standard Edition.

 

Script Optimization

The Script Optimizer is an AI-powered code review feature built directly into the Groovy script editor in Cloud Integration. It analyzes an existing script against SAP Cloud Integration best practices, identifies quality issues and upgrade readiness problems, and generates both a detailed review report and a ready-to-use improved version of the script. The feature is accessible via the Optimize button in the top-right corner of the script editor.

Clicking Optimize triggers the analysis. The editor switches to a split view — the original script remains on the left, while the AI-Generated Review Report appears on the right:

 

 

The report gives a structured breakdown of all identified issues. Each finding is numbered, named, and explained in detail — what the problem is, why it matters in the context of SAP Cloud Integration, and what needs to change. Typical findings include KBA violations such as loading the full message payload into memory as a String instead of using a streaming interface, missing error handling for JSON parsing, inefficient null checks, and patterns that are not compatible with current Groovy versions.

 

 

At the end of the report, the AI provides a fully rewritten version of the script that addresses all identified issues:

The improved script can be copied and applied directly in the editor. The report itself can also be downloaded, which is useful for documentation purposes or when reviewing changes with a team.

 

 

The Problems panel below the editor provides two tabs — Upgrade Readiness Checks and General Script Checks — giving a structured overview of all findings before stepping through the full report.

Availability: SAP Integration Suite Enhanced Edition, not on Standard Edition.

 

 

AI Adapter

The AI Adapter is a dedicated, native adapter type in SAP Integration Suite that enables an Integration Flow to call an AI service as a standard step within the integration process. Rather than building custom HTTP calls to AI backends, the adapter provides a structured, purpose-built configuration for AI service communication — making capabilities such as text generation, content analysis, and classification a native part of your integration flows.

The adapter is available in the standard adapter type list when adding a receiver channel in the Integration Flow Designer:

 

 

 

After selecting AI as the adapter type, you choose the message protocol that matches your target AI service:

 

 

  • Two variants are available. AICore-GenAIHub connects to SAP AI Core via the Generative AI Hub — the right choice when working within the SAP BTP ecosystem. Custom-OpenAI-REST connects to any OpenAI-compatible REST endpoint, covering custom model deployments or third-party providers that follow the OpenAI API specification.
  • Connection Tab

    The Connection tab defines how the adapter authenticates against and reaches the AI service.

    Address — the API endpoint URL of the AI service. For SAP AI Core, this is the API URL from your AI Core service key. For a custom deployment, it is the base URL of your OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

    OAuth2 Client Credentials — references a credential entry stored in the Integration Suite security material (Monitor → Security Material). You create this entry beforehand using the client ID and client secret from your AI Core service key, or the API key for your custom endpoint. The adapter uses this to authenticate each request.

    Connection Timeout / Response Timeout — both default to 60,000 ms. Depending on prompt complexity and model response time, it may be necessary to increase these values.

 

 

 


The Processing tab defines what is sent to the AI service and how the call is structured.

Operation: set to Orchestration - Completion, the standard mode for sending a prompt and receiving a generated text response.

Orchestration Deployment ID: the deployment ID of your AI scenario in SAP AI Core, available in the AI Core cockpit once a deployment has been created and started. It identifies which model configuration handles the request.

AI Resource Group:  the resource group within SAP AI Core, typically default unless dedicated groups have been configured for different use cases or teams.

Request Payload Source: UI Configurable means the prompt is defined directly within the adapter. The alternative passes the prompt dynamically via the incoming message, which is useful when the prompt content changes at runtime.

Model: the language model to use, for example gpt-4o-mini. Available models depend on what has been deployed in your AI Core instance or what your custom endpoint supports.

Role: the role context for the prompt. user represents the party sending the request, assistant the AI persona. For most integration scenarios, user is the appropriate choice.

Input: the prompt text sent to the AI model. The placeholder ${body} injects the current message payload dynamically into the prompt at runtime. This allows integration data — an error message, a purchase order, a customer record — to be passed directly to the AI without any additional scripting.

Availability: SAP Integration Suite Enhanced Edition and on Standard Edition.

 

Summary

The four features described in this post address AI support across the full iFlow development lifecycle. Artifact Generation provides a structured starting point for new integrations. Flow Design Proposal guides step selection during active modeling. Script Optimization delivers automated code review and improvement for existing Groovy scripts. The AI Adapter enables AI service calls as a native runtime step within any integration flow.

All four features are part of the Premium Edition of SAP Integration Suite and are generally available. Further AI capabilities — including the AI Message Monitoring Agent, Agent Orchestration, and the Error Analyzer — will be covered in a follow-up post.

entry.title: SFTP adapter file picking in Cpi
entry.content:
I configured an SFTP adapter to pick up a CSV file from the AL11 directory, where the file is generated from the backend. However, the file was not getting picked up. To troubleshoot, I tested by sending a sample CSV file from CPI to the same AL11 folder, and this demo file was successfully picked up by the SFTP adapter.

I also tried manually uploading a file into the AL11 directory, but that file was not picked up either. Although the iFlow deployment is successful and polling is working as expected, the issue is that only the file sent from CPI is being picked up, while the backend-generated and manually uploaded files are not.

Could you please help identify if this issue is related to Integration Suite, the backend system, or the AL11 directory configuration?

entry.title: SAP CPI and SFTP Adapter
entry.content:
I configured an SFTP adapter to pick up a CSV file from the AL11 directory, where the file is generated from the backend. However, the file was not getting picked up. To troubleshoot, I tested by sending a sample CSV file from CPI to the same AL11 folder, and this demo file was successfully picked up by the SFTP adapter.

I also tried manually uploading a file into the AL11 directory, but that file was not picked up either. Although the iFlow deployment is successful and polling is working as expected, the issue is that only the file sent from CPI is being picked up, while the backend-generated and manually uploaded files are not.

Could you please help identify if this issue is related to Integration Suite, the backend system, or the AL11 directory configuration?

entry.title: SAP DRC - Electronic Invoicing for Italy : ItalyReceiveNotification without SNI
entry.content:
Hello 

I am currently implementing SAP DRC - Electronic Invoicing for Italy.
We successfully managed to send documents to SdI, but unfortunately we are facing an issue with the ItalyReceiveNotification process. When SdI calls this service, they receive an HTTP 500 error. This could be related to the fact that SdI does not support SNI.

I found a forum discussion that refers to this issue: https://forum.italia.it/t/problema-test-interoperabilita/45870/7 
But I have not been able to find a concrete solution. I would like to understand whether SAP can resolve this issue on its side, or whether it must be addressed by our network team, for example by implementing a reverse proxy.

entry.title: Integration suite API that retrieves the message size
entry.content:
Hello

We need a standard SAP Integration Suite API that allows us to retrieve the size of the sent payload.

Currently, we are using the API at https://api.sap.com/api/MessageProcessingLogs, but it doesn't provide the size value.

Can you tell us if this is the correct API or what procedure we should follow to extract the payload size?

Our idea was to create a Groovy step within iFlow that adds a custom property to the log for the size. Please confirm or deny if this would be the correct approach.

entry.title: BTP IntegrationSuite SalesfrocePubSub adapter limits/costs
entry.content:
We are currently implementing integrations between Salesforce and SAP ECC using SAP Integration Suite / CPI and the Salesforce Pub/Sub adapter.

Our current design is event-driven, where Salesforce outbound interfaces publish events to Salesforce Pub/Sub queues/topics, which are then consumed by CPI and forwarded synchronously to SAP ECC through XI/proxy interfaces.

At the moment, the expected landscape would include approximately 20 different Salesforce event queues/topics, which would likely translate into around 20 Salesforce Pub/Sub adapter consumer instances/connections running in CPI.

We would like to better understand the potential operational and performance implications of this architecture on the SAP BTP Integration Suite tenant.

Specifically, we would appreciate guidance regarding:

  • Expected impact of multiple concurrent Pub/Sub adapter subscriptions on CPI runtime resources
    Memory/thread/worker consumption considerations for persistent gRPC streaming connections
  • Recommended sizing or scalability limits for Salesforce Pub/Sub consumers
  • Any known limitations, recommendations, or customer experiences regarding large-scale Salesforce Pub/Sub adapter usage

entry.title: Integration Suite sharing within company group - how to organize content
entry.content:
Hi all,

I'm working for a company which is part of a group. Until now, each company of the group uses several CPIs on NEO. We will need to migrate to Integration Suite now. For costing reasons, there is one global account owned by the group and shared with all companies. The Integration Suite landscape will consist of two subaccounts (test and prod). These subaccounts will be shared with all companies of the group due to costing reasons.

As same IS subaccounts will be used by various companies, administration and development as well as monitoring will be handled by different people at different locations and working for different companies. If we do this in an uncontrolled manner, it will become a mess. 

We need to find a way on how to organize all the content, artifacts, partner details etc. in a meaningful manner. For this, I want to define some governance of how to organize artifacts, naming conventions, authorization rules etc.

As I'm quite new to IS, I'm struggling with defining such a governance. I found lots of learning content but most of them are somewhat of fundamental. Create one project, create first flow and that's it.
What I've learned so far is that there is no hierarchy within integration projects, like we do have in ABAP packages. As projects are the most fundamental part of organizing content, I wonder if we should organize it by company, integration scenario (CPI for C4C, C4s, S/4, non-SAP), business area (Sales, Production) or in a different way?

I didn't found any best practices and for this, I want to ask you to point me into the right direction. How do you handle complex integration landscapes? Do you have some link to a good resource to best practices?

entry.title: MASS & XK99 not working
entry.content:
Dear Team,

 

We are not able to change some fields for vendor 

ex. LFA1-STCD5

Tax Number 5 we are not able to chnage or update new by mass
